speaker: interviewer
text: --November 1995. You were telling me about going back to Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, when-- we were-- actually, we-- it-- there were not actually wagons. There were just platforms, you know, like open-- because it was terribly, terribly, terribly hot. And we were terribly sunburned when we came to the border-- Czech and Polish border. There were trains bringing the war prisoners, and prisoners from concentration camps, and so on. Everybody was get terribly thirsty. So they were getting drinks. And we came too. And we were told-- we-- we were so thirsty. And we were told, you are Jews-- no water for Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A person from Red Cross. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After that? Well, we went to Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We couldn't get into our home, because it was occupied. And the law said we have to go through court. It was our property-- I mean, my parents' property. But it had to-- we had to go to the Red Cross and register, and register with the Jewish agency, and try to find somebody-- so a long process. But we couldn't just go into our place-- my parents' place-- and say, leave. Because we are the ones that lived here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were wandering around. And we stayed-- first night we stayed with Polish people that saved my sister-in-law's niece-- little girl. And we stayed there again.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next day, Peter was walking in the street, and-- with me-- and I met this mother of my friend with whom I went to school. And we were very befriended. Now she told me that her daughter, my friend, is alive. She was in the-- at the end of the war-- in the underground somewhere. Because in some places, the war ended earlier than the 8th of May. And she said, although they had two rooms there, and there are about 30 people, well, 2 more won't make much difference. So we went there, and we stayed with them. And then Peter was-- again, went to the Jewish agency to find out whether, you know, somebody is there. And he met a lady-- a girl-- that I shared a bed with-- bed-- that's very-- it sounds very luxurious-- the bunker with.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, what are you doing here? I am in Danzig. Because she was in Stutthof.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am in Danzig. Everything is ruin-- in ruins. But if you are lucky to find a flat, you have got a flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Peter went to have a look. He found-- first-- look. Peter is not-- we are not people that pushy-- are pushy, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody-- some Kashubians-- you don't know what they are. They are 1/2 Germans, 1/2 Polish. They are poor people, because for Germans, they are Polish. For the Poles, they are Germans. Well, her husband was captured by the Nazi-- by the Russians-- sorry. And she had two rooms. So she let us stay in one room there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in Danzig for?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Danzig, till 1952, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your son? Where was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Danzig. Yes, he was born in Danzig.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And until he was born, I didn't see any reason for living-- couldn't come to terms with what was going-- the-- the first impression-- when we got back to Cracow, I could not believe it. I could not accept it. The cafes are open. Cinemas are open. People get dressed up. All the shops are open. Hair-- people go to hair dressers. I-- I couldn't sort of put together, the two. And I was so depressed. I had to-- I had to go to the doctors. And I just didn't-- it was worthless for me to live. And of course, my brother wasn't much better, you know, Peter. So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --and one-- When was your son born?

speaker: interviewee
text: --day, the doctor tells me, you are pregnant. And I said, thank you very much. And he said, don't thank me. Thank your-- thank your husband. And quite frankly, I said, I don't want to bring children into this world. And he said, well, I can't perform an abortion. But if you want, on medical grounds, you have to see a doctor. And so the doctor said, yes, I am very weakened, and undernourished, and all that. And so I was making the appointments for an abortion. But each time I had to go, I cried. And I canceled it again. And I made another, and another, and another, until it-- it was too late. And I said, thanks God it's too late, because I would kill maybe somebody who will give new life to me. And that was true.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born on the 7th of June, 1947. It was the happiest moment in my life since the war came.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you left Danzig, where did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: From Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Israel. We were waiting 11 years for permission to leave Poland, because we still could read and write, you know. And at that time, there weren't many. And we had-- were were professional people. And even though I wasn't a-- a-- a member of the Communist Party, I was a woman. And I was the youngest. I had to take a high position. Because even with my background, which wasn't good. Because my father wasn't a thief. My mother wasn't a prostitute. So for the coming years, it wasn't a very good background. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But they needed me. And then I said, OK. I have done my job. So I changed it to another job. They said, no. You have to train the younger generation-- the younger generation-- the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you teaching them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you teaching them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the apprentices, you know, accountants. So-- and they-- and they were coming each time to sign that I want to join the Communist Party-- Peter too. And I said, I never join. My father was never joining any party, and I walk in his footsteps. I don't take sides. I don't join parties. I don't. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And also, if they wouldn't leave me alone, I said to myself, I have to tell them something that will leave me alone. And I said-- I had a good relationship with them, except that when I had one little glass of vodka, they already finished a bottle of vodka. And so I said, look. It wouldn't be right for me to join the party, because I have applied for a permission to leave this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to leave this country wasn't easy. I had to go every year to see the minister and cry my heart out. And the people I was working with, the Christians, were saying, you were at the wailing wall today? I said, yes. Because my eyes were swollen. They wouldn't let us out. And Peter-- oh, that's a long story too. So you went to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to Israel, finally.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you live in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four years, but I couldn't breathe, just like it's hard for me to breathe today. The hamsin there and the humidity here, it's-- but when we came here, the climate was different.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come here?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came here?

speaker: interviewer
text: To Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 15th of May, 1964.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what have you-- what have you done since you're in Australia? Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: What have I done?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went-- after two weeks, I went to work for Broadway Tailors. And the shops, I could not remember. They had 11 shops-- the Colony Shops. You heard of the Colony Shops? Menswear. And I worked there for 16 and 1/2 years in the accounts. But when my boss passed away, I couldn't see the future there anymore-- for the reasons that-- oh, well. He's-- it-- it was mismanaged later-- easy come, easy go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my boss left quite a bit. But I couldn't watch it being wasted. And I just spoke to his son-in-law. And I simply said, look. I really and truly-- this ship is sinking, and I don't want to be one of the rest to go down with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was giving him advices. He wouldn't take them. Then he-- a few years later, he went broke, of course. And he inherited a fortune. But that's not my business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you dream about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never stop. I never stop. Sometimes it's so beautiful, because I am at home with my parents, with my families, with my aunties and uncles. But sometimes it's in the camps. And sometimes I hear the marching of the-- the boots. And you-- I can't get rid of it. And I used to dream much more. I used to scream. I couldn't have the lights off. I had to have the lights on. I was frightened. That was still in Poland. But slowly, slowly, you know, I went back to normal. However, the dreams are there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And have-- have your experiences affected the way that you brought your-- brought up your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Have I experienced?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- what-- the things that happened to you, had-- did they affect the way that you brought up your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so-- very much so. Whether it was my upbringing, or whether it was-- I think that because of-- of why-- what I went through, perhaps maybe it made me a better person. You see, the war has divided the society into two. Some would fight to the last to obtain richness, to obtain material things, and go over dead bodies, but get it. The others are happy with what they have got-- enjoy every little bit-- as long as we are all happy, healthy, and live in peace. Now, to me, because I-- we had everything. And over night, we had nothing. Material things really don't matter to me. They are always arguing with me, my kids, that I give away everything to them. And I said, I don't need it. Your presence is the replacement of everything. So you might as well enjoy it. I like to give with a warm hand instead with a cold one. I have a friend who said to me once, oh, I love the dress you are wearing. And she said it a few times. And I said, have it. It was a new dress, but she liked it so much. So I said, have it. So then she said to me, I'm afraid to say something. You are worrying that I like it, because you will immediately let me have it. Because not-- it doesn't mean a thing to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you have the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- oh, sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Carry on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go-- no, I mean, I-- I do care about aesthetics, and you know, and to-- to be presentable, and be clean, and my house to be clean, and so on. But nothing much matters. It's just my family. And, of course, if I can do something for anybody else, with pleasure. But as my father used to say, if you give something to somebody, make sure it stays between you and the receiver.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you have a message for your son, and for his wife and children?

speaker: interviewee
text: A message. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] The message is that you can't appreciate health and freedom unless you have lost it. So I hope they never lose it. I'll pray for it always, for as long as I live, that they don't have to experience it. And take my words as being of true meaning-- that nobody can appreciate freedom and health until they have at once lost it and regained it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this, I'm trying to always convey to everybody, even with Australians. They'll come. My neighbor will come and worry about a stupid plant that died or something. Or their friend said something, or she-- at the races. I said, you healthy? You can do and go where you want? Yes. That's what's important. And she said, of course, Syda, you are so right. To hell with them, she says. Is there anything else you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I-- it's regrettable that for whatever purpose we are doing it-- I am aware of the purpose-- but I am sure that there will still be people in the world like what's his name-- the man from England-- Irving-- who will deny it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how can anybody denying-- deny it if there are so much-- there is so much proof? The ones who want to deny it, and want to keep it-- keep it in hiding, or-- or deny it for money, will meet the same-- sometimes in future-- the same sufferings-- maybe of a different kind-- but the same feeling of being persecuted.

speaker: interviewee
text: Although, I am not for it. Nobody should be persecuted for their color, religion. I don't believe in it. We don't come into the world by our own invitation, and we can't choose the religion, or color, or race we are born into. Once we are in it, we have to preserve it and be happy with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this photo of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bluma Hinde Florenz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kolomyia, which is now-- it was, before the war, Poland. Before the first war it was-- the Austro-Hungarian emporium, I imagine. And now it would be Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she when the photograph was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 17 years. And this is a picture when she got dressed up to be engaged to my father, to get married to my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The man in the top row, on the left-hand side, is my mother's brother, Zalman Leiner. The lady next to him is my mother's sister-in-law, my Auntie Ida Leiner. In the next row on the left, that-- that's myself. I think I am there about 10 years old. I-- maybe it says on the other side. Next to it is my mother. And next to my mother is her sister, Gusta Leiner. With her is her little boy, called Eric Leiner. The last row from the bottom, is my sister-- from the left-hand side-- is my sister Eda Florenz. In the middle is my little cousin, Lusiek Leiner. And the girl next to him is my cousin, Miriam Leiner.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the photograph taken? When? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe there is a date there. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Probably 1932--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --if you were 10.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute. Is there a date?

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll look at it afterwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: If it will be on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it must have been around '32. And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Mosciska, which is-- I won't tell you much. It's close to Lviv. It's now the Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank--

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my cousins. This is Olek Heller and Hanka Heller. Olek Heller, during the war and before the war, was studying in Cambridge, at university. And he survived the war. His sister, however, Hanka Heller, who was a beautiful blonde girl with blue eyes, was exterminated-- killed brutally by the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took out her eyes and ripped out her blonde hair-- beautiful blonde hair. She was 15 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK. Who's-- who's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there are two pictures here the-- of me. The right-- the picture on the right-hand side actually expresses what was going on in my heart at the time when the picture was taken-- I would say about eight months after the end of the war. However, my photographer wasn't happy with the look. So here I am smiling-- But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: --just smiling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the photograph--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who's in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left-hand side is my sister-in-law, Eleana Florenz. In the center is my brother, my late brother. And on the right-hand side, that's myself. And we were strolling through the streets of Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your brother's name who--

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name is Beno-- what? Yes? Was Beno Florenz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this picture-- the picture, and the reason for the picture, were our-- my greatest joy in my life, when my-- our son got married. And we-- that's the day I will remember for as long as I am sane.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were they married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: When were they married?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were married on the 6th of September, 1987, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sydney, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Sydney.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your son's and his wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter-in-law's name is Susan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacob.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Tell me about this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's my pride and joy. These are my two grandsons. The one on the left is Benjamin, who will be seven years old in March. And the little one is David, who will be four years in January. And there's absolutely nothing I'll-- in this world I love more than those two little puppies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, that's my husband and myself on somebody's wedding or bar mitzvah. I can't remember. But it obviously was a while ago, because I still look myself. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Peter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was taken in Sydney?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Taken in Sydney.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Melbourne, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Melbourne.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was taken in Melbourne at a wedding, or bar mitzvah, or something. Actually, it can be seen in the back, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's on.

speaker: interviewee
text: These-- these are pages from Schindler's List-- literally, Schindler's List-- just as the name of the movie. They were obtained from the German authorities. On one page is my haftling number, which is prisoner number-- 76449. On the other one is Peretz Selinger, who is my husband, and his prisoner number-- 69300.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Would you like to introduce your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Could you-- can you speak a bit louder?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to introduce your family, Mrs. Selinger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, with pleasure. This is my husband, Peretz Selinger, known as Peter Selinger. This is my son, Jake Selinger, and my daughter-in-law, Susan Selinger, and my dearest, sweetest grandsons, Benjamin Selinger-- Benjamin Joshua Selinger, and little David Jeremy Selinger. And that is all my richness.

speaker: interviewer
text: Peter, would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We went through a very terrible time-- hard to believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jake?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've-- [SIGHS] I guess I've grown up with the stories. I've heard them since early childhood. I guess they were just stories until I read the book, where other people have made statements and told stories which are identical to my parents. That's when everything started falling into place. But I guess the reality is that mom and I were fortunate enough to be in Poland two years ago. And we visited Cracow, and the site of the Plaszow camp, and Auschwitz. And that's really when it all fell into place. And it's very hard to believe that people could manage to survive what happened in those places. I think they must be very strong individuals, and I really admire them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would the boys like to say anything to their granny about the-- don't you want to tell your granny that you love her? What about just saying, I love you, granny?

speaker: interviewee
text: Come on. Tell grandma you love her. No?

speaker: interviewee
text: You do love your grandma! What about--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: --saying you love grandma's schnitzels? Do you like grandma's schnitzels?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yuck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, David! [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they're a bit shy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
